title: Subarray Sum Equals K
description: Given an array of integers nums and an integer k, return the total number of subarrays whose sum equals k.

examples.explanation: Three subarrays sum to 2: [1,1] starting at index 0, [1,1] starting at index 1... wait, n=3. Subarrays: [1,1] (i=0,1), [1,1] (i=1,2). That is 2. Actually nums=[1,1,1] k=2 has answer 2. LeetCode lists this as answer 2.

intuition: The brute force — check every subarray by trying all start and end indices — is O(n^2) or O(n^3). The key insight is the prefix sum property: sum(i..j) = prefix[j] - prefix[i-1]. So if we want sum(i..j) == k, that means prefix[j] - prefix[i-1] == k, which means prefix[i-1] == prefix[j] - k. Reframe it: as we walk through the array maintaining a running prefix sum, we want to know how many times we have seen (current_prefix_sum - k) before. If we have seen that value m times, there are m subarrays ending at the current index that sum to k. A hash map stores prefix_sum -> count_of_times_seen. The map is initialized with {0: 1} before we start — this handles the case where the subarray starts at index 0 (prefix[i-1] = 0, and that prefix 'appeared once' before any element was processed). Every step: add current element to prefix sum, check if prefix_sum - k is in the map, add its count to the answer, then record the current prefix sum in the map.
why_initialize_zero_one: The initialization {0: 1} is critical and commonly misunderstood. It represents the empty prefix — the state before index 0. If the entire subarray from index 0 to j sums to k, then prefix[j] - k = prefix[j] - k = 0. We need 0 to already be in the map with count 1 for this to be found. Without it, any subarray starting at index 0 that sums to k would be missed.
why_not_sliding_window: Sliding window works for subarray sum problems only when all numbers are POSITIVE (or all non-negative). When all elements are positive, the window sum is monotonically increasing as you extend right and decreasing as you shrink left — making the two-pointer convergence valid. This problem allows negative numbers, so extending the window can decrease the sum and shrinking it can increase the sum. The monotonicity breaks and sliding window gives wrong answers. Prefix sum + hash map handles negatives correctly.

core_equation.definition: prefix_sum at index i = nums[0] + nums[1] + ... + nums[i]
core_equation.target: We want sum(nums[i..j]) == k, so: prefix_sum[j] - prefix_sum[i-1] == k
core_equation.lookup: For each j, compute lookup_diff = prefix_sum - k. Count how many times lookup_diff appeared as a prefix sum before index j.

approach.name: Prefix sum + hash map
steps: For each element nums[i], add it to prefix_sum.
If lookup_diff is in prefix_sum_dict, add prefix_sum_dict[lookup_diff] to ans.
Update prefix_sum_dict: increment the count for prefix_sum by 1. Use .get(prefix_sum, 0) + 1 to handle first occurrence.

code.solution:
from typing import List

class Solution:
    # Intuition: find prefix sum, keep track of it and reduce it with k
    # no need for weird formulas — utilize the natural property of addition of elements

    def subarraySum(self, nums: List[int], k: int) -> int:

        # initialize the prefix sum and dict
        prefix_sum = 0
        prefix_sum_dict = {0: 1}  # 0 is the first prefix sum before we start
        ans = 0

        for i in range(len(nums)):
            prefix_sum += nums[i]
            lookup_diff = prefix_sum - k

            if lookup_diff in prefix_sum_dict:
                ans += prefix_sum_dict.get(lookup_diff)

            prefix_sum_dict[prefix_sum] = prefix_sum_dict.get(prefix_sum, 0) + 1

        return ans

        # Time Complexity:  O(n) — one pass, O(1) hash map operations
        # Space Complexity: O(n) — hash map stores at most n+1 distinct prefix sums

complexity.time: O(n) — single pass through the array. Each iteration does O(1) work: one addition, one subtraction, one hash map lookup, one hash map update.
complexity.space: O(n) — the hash map stores at most n+1 distinct prefix sum values (one per index plus the initial 0).

update_order_matters: The map update (recording current prefix_sum) must happen AFTER the lookup. If you update first and then look up, a subarray of length 0 (same start and end index) could match itself — inflating the count incorrectly for k=0 cases. Always: look up first, then record.
python_gotchas: prefix_sum_dict.get(prefix_sum, 0) + 1 is the idiomatic way to increment a counter with a default of 0. Alternatively use collections.Counter or collections.defaultdict(int) to avoid the .get() default entirely.

prefix_sum_dict.get(lookup_diff) without a default returns None if the key is missing. The 'if lookup_diff in prefix_sum_dict' guard prevents this, but if you remove the guard, ans += None raises a TypeError. Using .get(lookup_diff, 0) without the guard is also safe and more concise.
The order of operations in the loop is critical: add to prefix_sum, then lookup, then update the dict. Updating the dict before the lookup inflates the answer for k=0.
Using 'for num in nums' instead of 'for i in range(len(nums))' is more Pythonic here — you never use the index i. Change prefix_sum += nums[i] to prefix_sum += num.
defaultdict(int) from collections auto-initializes missing keys to 0, eliminating all .get() calls: prefix_sum_dict[prefix_sum] += 1 works directly.

cleaner_version.code:
from collections import defaultdict

def subarraySum(self, nums, k):
    prefix_sum_dict = defaultdict(int)
    prefix_sum_dict[0] = 1
    prefix_sum = 0
    ans = 0
    for num in nums:
        prefix_sum += num
        ans += prefix_sum_dict[prefix_sum - k]
        prefix_sum_dict[prefix_sum] += 1
    return ans

related_problems.title: Two Sum (LC 1)
related_problems.relation: Same hash map lookup pattern: store seen values and check for a complement. Here prefix_sum - k is the complement; in Two Sum, target - num is the complement.

related_problems.title: Continuous Subarray Sum (LC 523)
related_problems.relation: Uses the same prefix sum idea but checks if prefix_sum % k == prefix_sum[j] % k (i.e., same remainder) instead of exact equality. Map stores remainder -> index.

related_problems.title: Subarray Sums Divisible by K (LC 974)
related_problems.relation: Direct generalization: count subarrays whose sum is divisible by k. Uses prefix_sum % k as the key instead of prefix_sum itself.

related_problems.title: Path Sum III (LC 437)
related_problems.relation: Same prefix sum + hash map trick applied to a binary tree instead of a linear array. The map stores prefix sums seen along the current root-to-node path.

related_problems.title: Maximum Size Subarray Sum Equals k (LC 325)
related_problems.relation: Same prefix sum equation, but instead of counting subarrays, you want the longest one. Store prefix_sum -> first_index_seen and compute j - map[prefix_sum - k].

mcqs.question: What is the core mathematical insight that makes this O(n) solution work?
options: sum(i..j) = prefix[j] * prefix[i-1], so we check for a product
sum(i..j) = prefix[j] - prefix[i-1] = k, which rearranges to prefix[i-1] = prefix[j] - k. For each j, count how many previous prefix sums equal prefix[j] - k.
The array is sorted, enabling binary search for the complement
sum(i..j) = k means nums[i] + nums[j] = k for the boundary elements
mcqs.explanation: The prefix sum formula sum(i..j) = prefix[j] - prefix[i-1] is the foundation. Setting this equal to k and rearranging: prefix[i-1] = prefix[j] - k. This converts 'count subarrays summing to k' into 'count earlier prefix sums equal to current_prefix - k' — a hash map lookup.

mcqs.question: Why is the hash map initialized with {0: 1} before the loop starts?
options: 0 is a placeholder to prevent KeyError on the first iteration
It represents the empty prefix (state before index 0). When a subarray starting at index 0 sums to k, lookup_diff = prefix_sum - k = 0. Without {0:1} in the map, this subarray would not be counted.
It handles the case where nums[0] == 0
It ensures the hash map is never empty so .get() always returns a value
mcqs.explanation: Consider nums=[3], k=3. After processing index 0: prefix_sum=3, lookup_diff=0. If {0:1} exists, ans += 1 — correct, [3] is a valid subarray. Without initialization, lookup_diff=0 is not in the map and the subarray is missed. The {0:1} seed represents 'before any element, we have seen prefix sum 0 exactly once.'

mcqs.question: Why does sliding window NOT work for this problem?
options: Sliding window cannot handle arrays longer than 1000 elements
Sliding window requires a fixed window size; this problem has variable-size subarrays

Sliding window works only when the array is non-negative. Negative numbers break the monotonicity assumption: extending the window can decrease the sum and shrinking it can increase it, so left/right pointer decisions have no valid direction.
Sliding window cannot count occurrences, only find a single match
mcqs.explanation: Two-pointer sliding window relies on: extending right increases sum, shrinking left decreases sum. This monotonicity holds only for non-negative arrays. With negatives, adding an element can decrease the running sum — you can never decide which direction to move the pointer. The prefix sum + hash map approach handles negatives naturally because it considers all subarray sums algebraically, not through window expansion.

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity and why?
options: O(n^2) — two nested loops are required to consider all subarrays
O(n log n) — hash map operations take O(log n) each
O(n) — single pass with O(1) hash map operations (lookup and insert are O(1) average)
O(n * k) — the lookup depends on the value of k
mcqs.explanation: One pass through n elements. Each iteration: one addition (O(1)), one subtraction (O(1)), one hash map lookup (O(1) average), one hash map insert/update (O(1) average). Total: O(n). Hash map operations are O(1) average due to Python's hash table implementation.

mcqs.question: Why must the hash map update (recording current prefix_sum) happen AFTER the lookup?
options: Order does not matter — the result is the same either way

If you update before looking up, for k=0 the current prefix_sum equals lookup_diff (prefix_sum - 0 = prefix_sum). You would find and count the current index's own prefix sum as a valid previous occurrence, inflating the answer with phantom zero-length subarrays.
Updating first causes a KeyError on the first iteration
Python dictionaries are read-only during iteration
mcqs.explanation: For k=0: lookup_diff = prefix_sum - 0 = prefix_sum. If you add prefix_sum to the map BEFORE looking up, you find the entry you just added — counting a zero-length subarray as valid. A subarray must have at least one element. Update-after-lookup ensures you only count subarrays that started at a strictly earlier position.

mcqs.question: What does prefix_sum_dict[prefix_sum] represent at any point in the loop?
options: The value of prefix_sum at that index
The number of times prefix_sum has been seen as a running prefix sum at any previous index (before the current one). It counts how many earlier positions had exactly this cumulative sum.
The number of elements summing to prefix_sum
The index at which prefix_sum was first seen
mcqs.explanation: prefix_sum_dict maps a prefix sum value to its frequency: how many times has this exact running total appeared as we processed elements left to right. When lookup_diff has count m in the dict, it means there are m starting points where a subarray ending at the current index has sum exactly k.

mcqs.question: For nums = [1, -1, 1], k = 1, what is the answer?
options: 1 — only [1] at index 0
2 — [1] at index 0 and [1] at index 2
3 — [1] at index 0, [1,-1,1] (full array), and [1] at index 2
0 — no subarray sums to 1
mcqs.explanation: Trace: dict={0:1}, ps=0. i=0: ps=1, lookup=0 -> ans+=1 (ans=1), dict={0:1,1:1}. i=1: ps=0, lookup=-1 -> not in dict, dict={0:2,1:1}. i=2: ps=1, lookup=0 -> ans+=2 (ans=3), dict={0:2,1:2}. Answer=3. The three subarrays are [1] (i=0), [1,-1,1] (i=0-2), and [1] (i=2).

mcqs.question: What is the space complexity and what fills the hash map?
options: O(1) — only the running prefix_sum variable is needed
O(k) — space depends on the target value
O(n) — the hash map stores at most n+1 distinct prefix sum values: one initial {0:1} seed plus at most one new entry per element
O(n^2) — we store a prefix sum for every subarray
mcqs.explanation: In the worst case (all distinct prefix sums), each of the n iterations adds one new key to the map. Plus the initial {0:1}. Total distinct keys: at most n+1. Space is O(n). Note: if the array has many repeated prefix sums (e.g., lots of zeros), fewer distinct keys are added but counts grow — still O(n) total keys.

mcqs.question: For nums = [3, 4, 7, 2, -3, 1, 4, 2], k = 7, trace the first three iterations.
options: iter 1: ps=3, lookup=-4, miss. iter 2: ps=7, lookup=0 -> ans=1. iter 3: ps=14, lookup=7 -> ans=2.
iter 1: ps=3, lookup=4, miss. iter 2: ps=7, lookup=7, miss. iter 3: ps=14, lookup=0, ans=1.
iter 1: ps=7, lookup=0 -> ans=1 immediately at first element
iter 1: ps=3, lookup=-4, miss. iter 2: ps=7, lookup=0 -> ans=1. iter 3: ps=14, lookup=7 -> ans+=1 (ans=2) since 7 is now in dict.
mcqs.explanation: dict={0:1}. iter 1: ps=3, lookup=3-7=-4, not in dict. dict={0:1,3:1}. iter 2: ps=7, lookup=7-7=0, in dict with count 1, ans=1. dict={0:1,3:1,7:1}. iter 3: ps=14, lookup=14-7=7, in dict with count 1, ans=2. dict={0:1,3:1,7:1,14:1}. Two subarrays so far: [3,4] (sum 7) and [4,7] (sum... wait 3+4=7 and 3+4+7-3=... let me recheck. [3,4]=7 yes. [7]=7 yes at index 2. Both correct.

mcqs.question: nums = [1, 1, 1], k = 2. What is the answer and why does the {0:1} initialization matter?
options: 1 — only one subarray of two consecutive 1s exists
2 — [1,1] at indices (0,1) and [1,1] at indices (1,2)
3 — three subarrays summing to 2 exist
0 — k=2 requires at least two elements, which are not available
mcqs.explanation: dict={0:1}. i=0: ps=1, lookup=-1, miss. dict={0:1,1:1}. i=1: ps=2, lookup=0, hit count 1, ans=1. dict={0:1,1:1,2:1}. i=2: ps=3, lookup=1, hit count 1, ans=2. Return 2. The {0:1} at i=1 found the subarray [1,1] starting at index 0 (prefix 0 appeared once before index 0, meaning the subarray starts right at the beginning).

mcqs.question: What does lookup_diff = prefix_sum - k semantically represent?
options: The difference between the current element and k
The prefix sum value that must have existed at some earlier index for the subarray from that index+1 to the current index to sum to k
The number of elements that sum to k
The amount by which the current sum exceeds k
mcqs.explanation: If prefix[j] - prefix[i-1] = k, then prefix[i-1] = prefix[j] - k = lookup_diff. So lookup_diff is the target earlier prefix sum we need to have seen. Each occurrence of lookup_diff in the map corresponds to one starting point i where sum(i..j) = k. The count in the map tells us how many such starting points exist.

mcqs.question: Why does this approach correctly count subarrays with the same sum appearing multiple times?
options: It only counts each subarray once because indices are unique

The hash map stores frequencies, not just presence. If lookup_diff appears m times in the map, there are m distinct starting points that produce a subarray ending at the current index with sum k. ans += m adds all m of them in one operation.
It uses a set to deduplicate before counting
Repeated subarrays are handled by the {0:1} initialization
mcqs.explanation: Consider nums=[1,1,1], k=2. At index 2 (ps=3), lookup_diff=1. The map has {1:1} — count 1. At index 1 (ps=2), lookup_diff=0. Map has {0:1} — count 1. Both correctly count one starting point each. If instead prefix sum 1 had appeared twice (two distinct earlier points), the map would hold {1:2} and ans += 2 at once — correct, two subarrays end at the current index with sum k.

mcqs.question: nums = [0, 0, 0], k = 0. What is the answer?
options: 0 — k=0 is a degenerate case the algorithm does not handle
3 — [0], [0,0], [0,0,0] but we're missing some
1 — only the full array sums to 0
mcqs.explanation: All prefix sums are 0. dict={0:1}. i=0: ps=0, lookup=0, hit count 1, ans=1. dict={0:2}. i=1: ps=0, lookup=0, hit count 2, ans=3. dict={0:3}. i=2: ps=0, lookup=0, hit count 3, ans=6. Return 6. Subarrays: [0](i=0), [0](i=1), [0](i=2), [0,0](i=0-1), [0,0](i=1-2), [0,0,0](i=0-2). All six sum to 0. The update-after-lookup rule is critical here — otherwise count would be inflated further.

mcqs.question: How is prefix_sum_dict.get(prefix_sum, 0) + 1 different from prefix_sum_dict[prefix_sum] += 1?
options: They are identical in behavior
.get(prefix_sum, 0) + 1 returns a new value without modifying the dict — you must still assign it back. [prefix_sum] += 1 raises a KeyError if the key doesn't exist yet, unless using defaultdict.
.get() is O(log n) while [] access is O(1)
.get() only works for integer values; [] works for any type
mcqs.explanation: prefix_sum_dict.get(prefix_sum, 0) + 1 computes the new count but does NOT store it — you need prefix_sum_dict[prefix_sum] = prefix_sum_dict.get(prefix_sum, 0) + 1 to assign it back. On a plain dict, prefix_sum_dict[prefix_sum] += 1 raises KeyError on first occurrence. With defaultdict(int), += 1 works directly because missing keys default to 0. The code as written uses .get() with explicit assignment — correct and explicit.

mcqs.question: What happens with negative numbers in the array? Does the algorithm still work?
options: No — prefix sums can go negative which causes hash map errors
Yes — the prefix sum formula and hash map approach work correctly for any integer values including negatives. Negative numbers can create more distinct prefix sums but the algorithm is unchanged.
No — negative numbers require a different initialization than {0:1}
Yes but only if all negatives come before all positives
mcqs.explanation: The algorithm makes no assumption about the sign of elements or prefix sums. The hash map keys can be any integer — positive, negative, or zero. Negative elements can cause the running prefix_sum to decrease, creating prefix sums we might see again later. That is fine: the frequency count in the map handles it correctly. This is exactly why prefix sum + hash map is chosen over sliding window for this problem.

mcqs.question: Could you solve this with a brute force approach? What would the complexity be?
options: Yes — O(n) with a single pointer
Yes — O(n^2) by fixing each starting index and extending rightward, accumulating the sum and checking against k at each step
Yes — O(n^3) only — there is no O(n^2) brute force
No — the hash map is strictly required for correctness
mcqs.explanation: Brute force: for each start i from 0 to n-1, maintain a running sum starting from i. For each end j from i to n-1, add nums[j] to the sum and check if it equals k. Two nested loops, O(1) inner work: O(n^2) time, O(1) extra space. Correct but too slow for n=2*10^4. The O(n^3) variant recomputes the sum from scratch for each (i,j) pair instead of incrementally — also correct but slower.

mcqs.question: For nums = [-1, -1, 1], k = 0, what is the answer?
options: 0 — no subarray sums to 0
1 — only [-1,1] at indices (1,2) sums to 0... wait let me check. -1+1=0 yes. -1+-1+1=-1 no. -1 no. 1 no. Only one.
mcqs.explanation: dict={0:1}. i=0: ps=-1, lookup=-1, miss. dict={0:1,-1:1}. i=1: ps=-2, lookup=-2, miss. dict={0:1,-1:1,-2:1}. i=2: ps=-1, lookup=-1, hit count 1, ans=1. dict={0:1,-1:2,-2:1}. Answer=1. The subarray is nums[2..2]=... wait prefix[-1] appeared at i=0, so subarray is from index 1 to index 2: [-1,1] = 0. Correct.

mcqs.question: What is the Pythonic improvement to replace 'for i in range(len(nums)): prefix_sum += nums[i]'?
options: for i, nums[i] in enumerate(nums): prefix_sum += nums[i]
for num in nums: prefix_sum += num — iterate directly over values since the index i is never used elsewhere in the loop
prefix_sum = sum(nums) before the loop
for i in nums: prefix_sum += i
mcqs.explanation: The loop body uses nums[i] only to add to prefix_sum. The index i is never used for anything else (no nums[i-1], no bounds checking). Python's idiomatic form is 'for num in nums: prefix_sum += num'. This is cleaner and avoids the unnecessary range(len()) pattern. If you needed the index for something, enumerate(nums) would be appropriate.

mcqs.question: How would you modify this solution for LC 974 — Subarray Sums Divisible by K?
options: Replace k with k*2 in the lookup

Store prefix_sum % k as the key instead of prefix_sum. Two prefix sums with the same remainder mod k indicate a subarray between them divisible by k. Initialize dict with {0:1} still valid. Handle negative remainders by adding k: remainder = prefix_sum % k (Python handles this correctly already).
Use a sorted dict instead of a regular dict
Run the same algorithm twice and sum the results
mcqs.explanation: sum(i..j) divisible by k means prefix[j] - prefix[i-1] ≡ 0 (mod k), i.e., prefix[j] ≡ prefix[i-1] (mod k). So we store remainder = prefix_sum % k in the dict and look up the same remainder. Python's % always returns non-negative for positive k, so no special negative handling needed. The {0:1} initialization still correctly handles subarrays starting at index 0.

mcqs.question: How does this problem relate to Two Sum (LC 1)?
options: They are unrelated — one is about subarrays, one about pairs

Both use the complement lookup pattern in a hash map: store seen values and check if the needed complement exists. In Two Sum: for each num, look up target - num. Here: for each prefix_sum, look up prefix_sum - k. The hash map role is identical — O(1) lookup for a needed value.
This problem is strictly harder and uses a different algorithm entirely
Two Sum uses two pointers while this uses prefix sums — no algorithmic connection
mcqs.explanation: The core hash map pattern is the same. Two Sum: 'have I seen target - current_num before?' Store seen numbers. Subarray Sum: 'have I seen prefix_sum - k before?' Store seen prefix sums. In both cases you maintain a hash map of what you have seen and perform a complement lookup. Recognizing this connection makes both problems feel like variations of one idea.

mcqs.question: What would happen if you forgot to initialize {0:1} and instead started with an empty dict?
options: A KeyError would occur on the first iteration

All subarrays starting at index 0 that sum to k would be missed. For example, nums=[k], the single-element subarray [k] would not be counted. The output would be strictly less than or equal to the correct answer.
The first element would always be incorrectly counted
The algorithm would still be correct for all cases except when nums[0]==0
mcqs.explanation: Any subarray starting at index 0 and ending at index j has prefix[j] - prefix[-1] where prefix[-1] is conceptually 0. When lookup_diff = prefix_sum[j] - k = 0, the algorithm needs to find 0 in the map to count this subarray. Without {0:1}, 0 is not in the map on the first encounter and these subarrays are missed. The impact grows with how many valid subarrays start at index 0.

mcqs.question: nums = [1, 2, 3], k = 6. What is the answer and trace through the algorithm.
options: 0 — no subarray sums to 6
1 — only [1,2,3] (the full array) sums to 6
2 — [1,2,3] and [3,...] something
3 — three subarrays
mcqs.explanation: dict={0:1}. i=0: ps=1, lookup=1-6=-5, miss. dict={0:1,1:1}. i=1: ps=3, lookup=3-6=-3, miss. dict={0:1,1:1,3:1}. i=2: ps=6, lookup=6-6=0, hit count 1, ans=1. dict={0:1,1:1,3:1,6:1}. Answer=1. Only [1,2,3] sums to 6.

mcqs.question: Can this algorithm count the same subarray more than once, leading to overcounting?
options: Yes — if the same prefix sum appears multiple times, each occurrence is counted separately

No — each (i,j) pair representing a subarray is counted exactly once. Each occurrence of lookup_diff in the map corresponds to a distinct starting index i. No two entries in the map represent the same starting index. Counting each entry once is correct.
Yes — the {0:1} initialization double-counts subarrays starting at index 0
Yes — if k=0, every subarray is counted twice
mcqs.explanation: Each key-value pair in the map is prefix_sum_value -> frequency. Each unit of frequency corresponds to exactly one distinct earlier index where that prefix sum occurred. When we do ans += prefix_sum_dict[lookup_diff], we add the number of distinct starting points for valid subarrays ending at the current index. Each starting point is counted exactly once (when it was recorded). No double-counting.

mcqs.question: What is the connection between this problem and Path Sum III (LC 437) on a binary tree?
options: Both use BFS to traverse the structure

Both apply the prefix sum + hash map technique: maintain a running prefix sum from the root (or start of array), look up prefix_sum - k in a map of seen prefix sums. On a tree, you pass the map down via recursion and undo entries when backtracking (since each path is independent). The core equation prefix[current] - prefix[ancestor] = k is identical.
Both problems require sorting the input
Path Sum III only works for binary search trees; this problem works for any array
mcqs.explanation: The equation is the same: we want paths (root to node) where the sum along the path equals k, which means prefix[node] - k must exist as an earlier prefix sum. The difference is tree structure: each root-to-leaf is a separate 'array', so you use DFS and a mutable map that you increment when going deeper and decrement when backtracking (to avoid cross-path interference). Same algorithm, different traversal.

mcqs.question: If you needed to find the LENGTH of the longest subarray summing to k instead of counting all such subarrays, what would change?
options: Nothing — the same code works for both

Instead of storing prefix_sum -> count, store prefix_sum -> first_index_seen. For each j, if lookup_diff is in the map, compute length = j - map[lookup_diff] and update a max_length variable. Don't update the map if a key already exists (keep the earliest index for maximum length). This is LC 325.
Use a sorted map (like a balanced BST) to find the furthest match
Run the current algorithm twice: once forward, once backward, and take the max
mcqs.explanation: For counting: store frequency (each occurrence of prefix_sum matters). For maximum length: store the FIRST index where each prefix_sum appeared (earlier index = longer subarray from that point). When lookup_diff is found at map[lookup_diff] = earliest_index, length = current_j - earliest_index. Don't overwrite existing map entries — keeping the earliest index maximizes length. This is LC 325 Maximum Size Subarray Sum Equals k.
